title-group.article-title: Assessment of Ambient Multipollutant Exposure and Health Impacts Over India Using the Novel Satellite‐Based Multi‐Pollutant Index

article-meta.aff: 1 Centre for Atmospheric Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, New Delhi, India
2 Department of Energy, Environmental & Chemical Engineering, Washington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, MO, USA
3 Department of Health, Policy and Management, Korea University, Seoul, South Korea

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Ambient air pollutants are reported to have adverse health impacts, which could be better assessed by examining human exposure to multiple criteria pollutants or through a multi‐pollutant Air Quality Index (AQI). As compared to AQIs developed using in situ measurements of pollutants across sparse monitoring stations, satellite‐based products such as ambient PM2.5 or NO2 provide better spatiotemporal patterns. Here, we examine the long‐term (2005–2019) air pollution exposure and health impacts over India with the novel Satellite‐Based Multi‐Pollutant Index (SMPI). The high spatial resolution (1 km × 1 km) SMPI over India reveals pollution exposure hotspots in most states and districts over the Indo‐Gangetic Plain (IGP) and eastern states. The spatiotemporal patterns in SMPI exposure and its urban‐rural heterogeneity were different from either PM2.5 or NO2, highlighting the importance of multi‐pollutant indices to comprehensively assess air quality. Furthermore, we found a strong association (1.05, 95% UI: 1.025–1.075) between SMPI and odds of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), which increased with increasing co‐pollutant exposures [1.03 (1.018–1.042) to 1.184 (1.156–2.212)]. The SMPI—COPD association was significantly higher among males, middle class, tribes, and older sub‐populations, highlighting the need for future environmental policies prioritized for vulnerable population subgroups for larger health benefits.

sec.p: Keywords: air pollution, multi‐pollutant index, SMPI, satellite, health, India

abstract.title: Plain Language Summary
abstract.p: Exposure to ambient air pollutants have been reported to have severe public health impacts. Air pollution is better assessed by examining human exposure to multiple criteria pollutants or through a multi‐pollutant Air Quality Index (AQI). As compared to AQIs developed using in situ measurements of pollutants across sparse monitoring stations, satellite‐based products provide better spatiotemporal patterns. We assess the long‐term air pollution exposure in India using a Satellite‐based Multi‐pollutant Index (SMPI) using satellite derived surface PM2.5 and NO2. Our analysis shows air pollution hotspots in the districts of Indo‐Gangetic Plain (IGP) and Eastern states in India. The patterns of SMPI exposure across urban and rural areas were different from those of PM2.5 or NO2 alone. This emphasizes the need for multi‐pollutant indices to get a complete understanding of air quality. Our results show that SMPI—Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease association was significantly stronger among males, middle class, tribes, and older sub‐populations. This highlights the need for targeted environmental policies to protect vulnerable groups.

list-item.p: Satellite‐Based Multi‐Pollutant Index (SMPI) or similar multi‐pollutant indices may be more suitable for assessing air quality and related health impacts
Long‐term (2005–2019) SMPI analysis in India reveals the air pollution hotspots in the Indo‐Gangetic Plain and Eastern states
Strong association between SMPI exposure and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, with higher risks among males, middle‐class, tribal, and older populations

sec.p: Revised 2025 Oct 11; Received 2025 Mar 10; Accepted 2025 Oct 18; Collection date 2025 Dec.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Exposure to ambient air pollutants such as PM2.5 and NO2 have been reported to have severe public health impacts (Achakulwisut et al., 2019; Anenberg et al., 2022; Balakrishnan et al., 2019; Crouse et al., 2015; Hamra et al., 2015; Pandey et al., 2021). The Global Burden of Disease (GBD) study calculated 27.41 (17.69–36.25) and 0.021 (0.018–0.024) million Disability‐Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) in India attributable to PM2.5 and NO2 exposures, respectively (Murray, 2024). The adverse health impacts of these ambient air pollutants could be better assessed by simultaneously examining exposure to multiple criteria air pollutants (Billionnet et al., 2012; Coffman et al., 2024; Oakes et al., 2014). To our knowledge, studies based on India have only estimated the single pollutant impacts on various health outcomes (Chaudhary et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024; Huangfu & Atkinson, 2020; Nori‐Sarma et al., 2021; Rajak & Chattopadhyay, 2020; Spears et al., 2019). However, the combined effect of ambient air pollutants is poorly studied over India. Moreover, the nature of the combined exposure effect from these pollutants (whether it is additive, synergistic, antagonistic, or interactive manner) among the Indian population is unknown.

To incorporate multiple criteria pollutants for assessing deteriorated air quality, Multi‐pollutant Air Quality Index (MAQI) has been introduced in the past (Plaia et al., 2013). Similar indices such as Air Quality Health Index (AQHI) and Health‐based Air Quality Index (HAQI) have been formulated to estimate the impact of multi‐pollutants on public health (Hu et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2022). K. Kumar, (2022) conducted a comprehensive review of air quality indices used worldwide from 1960 to 2021, taking into account various factors such as the number of pollutants considered, method of calculation (linear vs. nonlinear), averaging time, mode of estimation (single pollutant vs. multi‐pollutant), and health effects. In India, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) formulated the National Air Quality Index (NAQI) and classified the air quality into six categories based on the maximum value of individual pollutant concentrations and their associated health effects (CPCB, 2014). However, this index has limitations in identifying regions with poorest air quality caused by multiple air pollutants (Gurjar et al., 2008). Moreover, the traditional indices rely on observational data from ground‐based monitoring sites (K. Kumar, 2022), where the stations are sparse and lack continuous databases at higher spatiotemporal resolutions (Brauer et al., 2019; Martin et al., 2019). Since the existing AQIs partially represent the population exposure due to insufficient in situ observations, an index representing continuous spatiotemporal variability in multi‐pollutant exposures is essential for assessing air quality and attributable health impacts. This will also help identify pollution hotspots that require prioritized policies and interventions for pollution mitigation.

Compared to AQIs calculated using in situ measurements of pollutants at sparse monitoring stations, satellite‐based remote sensing provides better continuous spatiotemporal coverage. The satellite‐based retrievals of Aerosol Optical Depth (AOD) and vertical column density are currently used to derive reliable global and regional surface concentrations of PM2.5 and NO2, respectively (Anenberg et al., 2022; Cooper et al., 2022a, 2022b; Dey et al., 2020; Katoch et al., 2023; N. Singh et al., 2024; van Donkelaar et al., 2021). Cooper et al. (2012) proposed a new metric to assess multiple pollutants, Satellite‐based Multi‐Pollutant Index (SMPI), utilizing satellite‐derived surface PM2.5 and NO2 data and scaling by pollutant concentration deviation from WHO guidelines. Although SMPI relies only on two key pollutants (PM2.5 and NO2), which are reliably estimated using satellite observations, this new metric provides better spatiotemporal observational coverage than metrics based on in situ data. To our understanding, no further studies examined global or regional air pollution exposure and related adverse human health impacts using SMPI as a metric.

Therefore, the broad objective of this study is to assess the potential of SMPI as a better metric compared to PM2.5 or NO2 to examine air pollution exposure and health impacts in a region (India, in this study). We first assessed air pollution exposure in India from 2005 to 2019 using the SMPI metric derived from satellite‐based ambient PM2.5 and NO2 data sets. We have used the global data sets for surface PM2.5 (van Donkelaar et al., 2021) and NO2 (Cooper et al., 2022a, 2022b), both estimated using satellites and chemical transport models (GEOS‐Chem) to ensure data consistency. We then estimated the associations between PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI with the odds of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) among the Indian population using demographic information derived from the national family health survey—5th round (NFHS‐5, 2019–2021). Furthermore, we estimated effect modification of SMPI across the NFHS‐5 survey participant's gender, age, wealth, caste, cooking fuel types, and resident types. Finally, we estimated the reduction in COPD prevalence if Indian states succeed to meet the clean‐air targets in the foreseeable future. The results from this study will highlight the need to have multi‐pollutant indices to assess air quality and provide the metric for further epidemiological research in India, incorporating multiple air pollutants together.

sec.title: Satellite‐Derived Ambient PM2.5
sec.p: We used a high resolution (0.01° latitude × 0.01° longitude) ground‐level fine particulate matter (PM2.5) annual mean data set for 2005–2019 from van Donkelaar et al. (2021), updated to version V5.GL.03. The data set is derived geophysically by relating AOD retrievals combined from three satellite instruments MODIS (Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer), MISR (Multi‐angle Imaging SpectroRadiometer), and SeaWiFS (Sea‐viewing Wide Field‐of‐view Sensor) to surface‐level PM2.5 concentrations using the chemical transport model: GEOS‐Chem. These geophysical PM2.5 estimates are enhanced using in situ measurements from >11,000 global sites, including >3,000 monitors over Asia. These monitors are used within a Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR) framework to predict and adjust for bias in the initial geophysical PM2.5, increasing the accuracy of the final hybrid PM2.5 used in this study. Within Asia, the cross‐validated annual R2 between the hybrid PM2.5 and ground‐based observations ranges from 0.66 to 0.74.

sec.title: Satellite‐Derived Ambient NO2
sec.p: We used satellite‐derived high‐resolution (0.01° latitude × 0.01° longitude) ground‐level nitrogen dioxide (NO2) data obtained from a recent study (Cooper et al., 2022a, 2022b). The annually averaged data set from 2005 to 2019 is derived from the Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) and TROPOspheric Monitoring Instrument (TROPOMI) satellite NO2 retrievals. The NO2 column densities are determined for a given year using OMI observations downscaled to a finer resolution using TROPOMI observations. Cooper et al. (2022a, 2022b) utilized the downscaled column densities to derive surface‐level concentrations through the GEOS‐Chem chemical transport model, constraining it with ground monitor observations. The long‐term changes observed in this satellite‐derived NO2 data exhibit general consistency with the trends noted from available observational data in India (Cooper et al., 2022a, 2022b) while it shows an underestimation by a factor of 4 for the year 2019 (Figure S3 in Supporting Information S1).

sec.title: Ancillary Data
sec.p: We used Global Human Settlement Layer (GHSL) level‐2 data, identifying each (1 km × 1 km) grid as one of the eight classes (Schiavina, Melchiorri, & Pesaresi, 2022; Schiavina, Melchiorri, Pesaresi, Politis, et al., 2022; Figure S1 in Supporting Information S1). The data is available in distinct years, such as 2005, 2010, and 2015. We combined three rural grids into a single rural grid and four semi‐urban and urban grids into a single urban grid in 2005, 2010, and 2015. We used population data from the World Research Program, which is available for all countries from 2000 to 2020 and represents the number of people per grid cell at a high resolution (1 km × 1 km) (WorldPop & Bondarenko, 2020). The projection of data sets is the Geographic Coordinate System, WGS84.

sec.title: SMPI Calculation and Exposure Analysis
sec.p: The satellite observations of surface concentrations can be used to define a SMPI. This approach is demonstrated by Cooper et al. (2012) by defining globally gridded SMPI products by scaling surface‐level PM2.5 (multi‐satellite) by NO2 concentration data (OMI satellite). We followed this approach and estimated SMPI over India using a recently available data set of satellite‐based surface PM2.5 data from van Donkelaar et al. (2021) and NO2 concentration data from Cooper et al. (2022a, 2022b). The following equation is used for the calculation of SMPI,

Here we refer to WHO Air Quality Guidelines 2021 (AQG) PM2.5 concentrations of 5 μg/m3 and NO2 concentrations of 10 μg/m3. The above AQGs are for the year 2021, which are different from that used in Cooper et al. (2012). In this study, we have also analyzed the long‐term SMPI values calculated using the Air Quality Guidelines (AQG) from the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, Central Pollution Control Board of India) for 2019, PM2.5 concentrations of 40 μg/m3 and NO2 concentrations of 40 μg/m3.

A long‐term analysis of SMPI is conducted from 2005 to 2019. The long‐term means of PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI are determined, and spatial patterns are analyzed. Population‐weighted SMPI is calculated, and long‐term exposure analysis is also carried out. The long‐term (2005–2019) SMPI exposure for each state and district is determined by sampling satellite data within the boundaries of shapefiles obtained from DIVA‐GIS. The SMPI exposure in each state is estimated, and states are ranked based on pollution levels. For further investigation, the study period is divided into three sets of 5‐year mean blocks (2005–2009, 2010–2014, 2015–2019). Exposure analysis is conducted for each state and union territory during these periods, as well as the difference in the exposure between these periods. Additionally, SMPI exposures in urban and rural areas are analyzed for different periods.

sec.title: Socio‐Demographic Information
sec.p: We used the demographic and health information from the 5th round (2019‐21) of the national family health survey (NFHS) for our analysis here. The NFHS is a nationally representative household survey measuring population demographics, household characteristics, nutrition, and health (majorly prioritizing rural residents), and child and maternal health (The DHS Program, 2018). NFHS has a two‐stage sampling design. At first, the districts were selected and then, the clusters were selected within the districts for the individual and household‐level questionnaires (census enumeration blocks in urban areas, villages in rural areas). The NFHS sampling was carried out by the International Institute of Population Sciences (IIPS) under the Demographic and Health Survey (DHS) curriculum. Each cluster was classified as urban or rural based on individuals' per‐capita income, ownership of household assets, average educational level, and the overall socioeconomic status of the demographic sub‐populations. Within districts, the rural strata were further divided into smaller substrata according to village population size and the proportions of scheduled caste (SC) and scheduled tribe (ST) subgroups.

The 2011 census was used as the sampling frame to identify Primary Sampling Units (PSUs), also referred to as survey clusters. In rural strata, villages were selected as PSUs, with clusters arranged beforehand by female literacy rates (aged 6 years and above). In urban strata, Census Enumeration Blocks (CEBs) were chosen as PSUs, arranged by the proportion of SC and ST subgroups. In the second stage, 22 households were randomly selected from each cluster through equal‐probability systematic sampling, based on a fresh household listing prepared during mapping operations. PSUs with 300 or more households were divided into segments of about 100–150 households, from which one segment was chosen for the survey. Detailed descriptions of the NFHS sampling design are available in the publicly accessible NFHS reports (The DHS Program, 2018). For the individual‐level survey, women aged 15–49 years and men aged 15–54 years were selected across the households. An identical questionnaire following standardized measurement methods was used to ensure comparability and compatibility across diverse sites and times. The detailed description of the survey methods is in the DHS‐7 user manual. NFHS‐5 covered 30,197 clusters from 707 districts of India. In the NFHS sampling design, villages in rural areas and CEBs in urban areas were selected as clusters using probability proportional to size (PPS), followed by systematic household sampling within each cluster. The base sampling weight for a household was defined as the inverse of its selection probability, and these weights were subsequently adjusted for household and individual non‐response within each stratum. The adjusted weights were then normalized so that the weighted totals matched the actual sample size at national or subregional levels. These cluster‐level weights ensured that survey estimates remained representative and unbiased across population subgroups (The DHS Program, 2018).

We carried out our analysis over a total of 825,954 individuals across India. For the health effect assessment, baseline information on participants' demographic characteristics (age, sex, wealth, caste, education, occupation, marital status, health insurance, resident and cooking‐fuel types), and lifestyle habits (smoking and drinking status) were obtained from the latest NFHS round. We obtained the demographic information from the NFHS questionnaire administered to individual levels (male and female) and at household level. For the wealth index, the sample is divided into five categories, richest, richer, middle class, poorer, and poorest, based on the ranks of standardized scores for each household possessing different types of socio‐demographic and economic assets (The DHS Program, 2018). We clubbed the richest and richer subgroups as rich, and poorer and poorest subgroups as poor, while keeping the middle‐class sub‐population as separate.

Among the Indian populace, high exposure to solid fuel‐sourced household air pollution has been linked with various non‐communicable disease (NCD) health outcomes (Chowdhury et al., 2019), so the type of cooking‐fuel used by households has been adjusted in this analysis. Liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), electricity, biogas or natural gas was clubbed together as clean‐fuel in the households; while kerosene or biomass fuel (straw/shrubs/grass, wood, animal dung, agricultural crops, charcoal, coal/lignite, and others) were classified as solid‐fuel. The survey captured the information of four levels of an individual's educational attainment as no education, primary, secondary, and higher educational level. The occupation is considered as one key confounder for health outcomes as the types of occupation determines the exposure level and probability of developing various diseases (Chaudhary et al., 2022). The occupational classes considered in this analysis were categorized as professionals, farmers or agricultural laborers, workers, households or non‐workers, and others. The smoking status and type of smoking were also captured as for the sub‐groups, where a person smoking cigarettes, bidi, or tobacco was categorized as smokers. We used other dichotomous variables as covariates in the analysis, such as availing health insurance, type of residence (urban/rural), and second‐hand smoke exposure (yes/no). The information of multiple dietary intakes (daily per‐capita beverage, served process food, and packed process food) were captured in the NSSO survey, which were integrated with the NFHS survey data sets across the common households. In both surveys, each household is given a unique ID, these IDs from NFHS and NSSO are merged together using the Hot‐Deck method (Joenssen & Bankhofer, 2012). We also included the information on marital status (married, unmarried, and widow/separates/divorced) into our adjusted models. And lastly, the models were adjusted for other fixed effects like cluster ID, month, year, state, and district. For the health outcome, we relied on the survey information of individuals' prevalence of COPD in our analysis, which was recorded as binary information (Yes or No) during the interview. We adjusted the cluster‐specific “sample weights” in the models to assess the cluster level variability on the association between chronic PM2.5 exposure and the odds of COPD.

sec.title: Multivariate Regression Model
sec.p: We extracted the long‐term (2015‐19) annual mean concentration of PM2.5, NO2, and their corresponding SMPI estimates for each survey cluster (defined as urban or rural) based on their geo‐locations (centroid). These geo‐locations were used to create geospatial buffers with a radius of 5 and 2 km for rural and urban clusters, respectively. For this assessment, it was assumed that respondents remained in their respective clusters during the survey period and were continuously exposed to the cluster‐level long‐term ambient PM2.5 concentration. This assumption allowed temporal alignment between the air pollution estimates and the demographic information of NFHS. Within the NFHS framework, buffer radii of 2 km for urban clusters and 5 km for rural clusters were conventionally applied to generate geospatial buffers. This convention reflected the higher density and smaller spatial extent of urban clusters as compared with the larger, more spatially dispersed rural clusters. The smaller urban buffer minimized the inclusion of surrounding non‐representative areas, whereas the larger rural buffer ensured that representative ambient conditions were captured (The DHS Program, 2018). The mean concentrations of the pollutants within each buffer region were extracted using ArcGIS (v3.8) software. We converted each level of air pollutants' concentration (PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI) into their respective percentile estimates (1st to 100th percentiles) using array.rank(pct = True) command in Python. We then merged the air pollutant estimates with the individual and household level demographic information from NFHS‐5 using the unique code of each NFHS cluster.

We used the individual‐level hierarchical logistic mixed effects model to assess the association between ambient PM2.5 and NO2 concentrations with the odds of COPD among the Indian populace, accounting for differential clustering effect. We adjusted both the pollutants, PM2.5 and NO2 in our model and assessed the effect estimates of one pollutant due to the moderation of the other by odds ratio (ORs, with 95% uncertainty intervals, UIs) for per percentile increase in pollutant estimates. Note that the survey respondents were not independent observations but were nested with the clusters, which served as the primary sampling units in the NFHS framework. To account for this hierarchical structure, we specified the cluster as a random intercept in our hierarchical logistic mixed‐effects models. This approach allowed us to control for unobserved within‐cluster similarities (e.g., shared environment or socio‐demographic characteristics) and to produce valid standard errors and effect estimates. We then estimated the association between SMPI exposure and the odds of COPD. However, in the later model, we did not adjust PM2.5 and NO2 estimates to avoid multicollinearity in the effect estimation. All the statistical analyses were performed using statsmodels.api‐0.14.0 module in Python, and statistical significance is decided by p‐value <0.05. We performed the following regression model to estimate association between the air pollution estimates and the health outcomes,

Here, Y
ij represents the binary health outcome (COPD) for the jth individual in ith cluster, α is the intercept, u
i is the random intercept corresponding to ith cluster with u
i ∼ N(0, σ
u
2); in the multi‐pollutant model, β
i is the regression coefficient for PM2.5 or NO2, adjusted for its co‐pollutant, and for SMPI; and γ represents the regression coefficients corresponding to all other confounders Xij. We examined whether multicollinearity exists between ambient PM2.5 and NO2, and other relevant covariates. Figure S2 in Supporting Information S1 depicted the correlation statistics between the covariates, we found lower to moderate correlation (r < 0.6, p < 0.05) between the pollutants and among the covariates; therefore, minimizing the potential impact of multicollinearity between these covariates within an epidemiological assessment. The exposure estimates were converted into percentile ranks (1st to 100th percentiles) across their full distributions. And then, the odds ratio (OR) was estimated by eβ for each percentile increase in pollutants' exposure and SMPI. The ORs therefore represent the relative change in COPD risk associated with a one‐percentile increase in exposure rank, rather than per unit (in μg/m3) increase in pollutants concentration. Note that this percentile scaling was applied to allow comparability between pollutants with different concentration ranges and units. We carried out three additional sets of analyses where we estimated the association between SMPI and the odds of COPD across different ranges of PM2.5 and NO2, respectively. In the 1st set of analysis, we stratified the data set into four different ranges of PM2.5, such as <25th percentile, 25th to 50th percentile, 50th to 75th percentile, and >75th percentile, while adjusting for NO2. Similarly, in the 2nd set, we stratified the data set into four such ranges of NO2, while adjusting for PM2.5. In the 3rd set of analysis, we stratified the data set into four such exposure ranges of both PM2.5 and NO2. We intended to investigate how the association between SMPI and COPD varies across the different exposure ranges of PM2.5 and NO2 separately and considering both the pollutants together. Thus, stratification refers to subgroup analyses by exposure ranges, not a different method of scaling. Finally, we used an interaction model to assess the effect modification of SMPI on the odds of COPD across participant's gender [male and female (reference)], age [25–35 (reference), 35–45, and over 45 years], wealth [rich (reference), middle class, and poor], caste [general (reference), other backward class (OBC), and scheduled caste and scheduled tribes combined as (SC + ST)], cooking fuel used in the household [clean‐fuel (reference) and solid‐fuel], and the type of residence [urban and rural (reference)]. Table S1 in Supporting Information S1 shows the regression estimates.

sec.title: Health Benefits Assessment
sec.p: We examined the expected reduction in COPD prevalence (health benefits) if the states meet three clean‐air targets in the foreseeable future: (a) if the mean ambient PM2.5 and NO2 exposures during NFHS‐5 were above national ambient air quality standards (NAAQS) of 40 μg/m3or any of the interim‐targets by World Health Organization (WHO) (35, 25, and 15 μg/m3 for PM2.5 and 30 and 20 μg/m3 for NO2) during NFHS‐5, first clean‐air target for the states would be to achieve their nearest exposure reduction target, for which we estimated the reduced SMPI values; (b) similarly, for the 2nd clean‐air target, we considered that the states would meet their next feasible exposure reduction target after achieving 1st clean‐air target, and we calculated the corresponding SMPI values; and finally, (c) we estimated the reduced SMPI while considering the states to achieve the WHO air quality guideline (AQG) of 5 and 10 μg/m3 for PM2.5 and NO2 respectively, in the foreseeable future. For the three exposure reduction scenarios, we first estimated the reduction in SMPI values at the state level. The attributable fraction was derived using the following relation:

Where RR=eloglog(OR)×ΔSMPI and OR is the estimated odds ratio for each percentile increase in SMPI exposure; and ΔSMPI is the difference in SMPI exposure across the three reduction scenarios with respect to the long‐term mean SMPI estimate during NFHS‐5. Then the expected reduction in state level COPD prevalence (HB) was calculated due to the reduction in SMPI exposure as: HB = AF × State level COPD prevalence during NFHS‐5. We also estimated COPD prevalence across the states during NFHS‐5 and its changes under the three exposure reduction scenarios.

sec.title: Spatiotemporal Variability of SMPI Exposure
sec.p: Figure 1 shows long‐term (2005–2019) annual mean surface concentrations of PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI over India. The highly populated Indo‐Gangetic Plain (IGP) region has the highest PM2.5 concentrations, primarily from anthropogenic and biomass burning emissions and low ventilation coefficient (Dey et al., 2020; N. Singh et al., 2021). The long‐term mean PM2.5 concentration is maximum over Delhi National Capital Region (NCR) with a value of 132 μg/m3. We found that 99.5% of districts in India (except some regions in Ladakh) fail to meet the WHO AQG (2021) of 5 μg/m3. The NO2 concentration is highest at some distinct hotspots in the Northern (e.g., Delhi, Ghaziabad) and Central‐Eastern (e.g., Chhattisgarh, West Bengal) regions. The nitrogen oxide emissions from transportation, industries, and thermal power plants lead to NO2 enhancements in these regions (N. Singh et al., 2023). The NO2 concentration over the hotspots is higher than 15 μg/m3, exceeding the WHO's AQG (2021) of 10 μg/m3. We found that satellite‐based NO2 for the year 2019 is underestimated by a factor of 4 compared to in situ observations over India (see Figure S3 in Supporting Information S1) even though Cooper et al. (2022a, 2022b) states that the long‐term trends in satellite‐derived NO2 data is consistent with trends observed from in situ data in India. Future studies should explore the persistent underestimate of satellite‐based NO2 products over India (e.g., N. Singh et al., 2024).The spatial distribution of SMPI (unitless) reveals significant enhancements in the IGP region, primarily attributed to PM2.5. Additionally, Central‐Eastern states exhibit elevated SMPI values due to NO2 hotspots. Notably, the SMPI values presented in Figure 1, based on the WHO AQG for PM2.5 and NO2, exceed 30 in both the IGP and Central‐Eastern India. However, when considering the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) 2019 AQG for PM2.5 and NO2, the SMPI values over these regions are an order of magnitude lower (Figure S4 in Supporting Information S1). Here, we followed the WHO AQG (Equation 1 and Figure 1) to be consistent with Cooper et al. (2012) and to follow global AQG standards to compare Indian air quality with other global regions, in future studies.

caption.p: Long‐term (2005–2019) annual mean surface concentrations of PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI over India. SMPI is calculated using Equation 1 based WHO AQG (2021) for PM2.5 and NO2. The PM2.5 and NO2 concentration are in μg/m3 and SMPI is unitless. Satellite‐based ambient PM2.5 and NO2 data are from van Donkelaar et al. (2021) and Cooper et al. (2022a, 2022b) respectively.

sec.p: Figure 2 shows the long‐term annual mean PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI exposure (population‐weighted mean concentration) for different states and UTs in India. The spatial maps of state‐wise exposure values are in Figure S5 of Supporting Information S1. The PM2.5 exposure is highest (>80 μg/m3) in states in the IGP region (Delhi, Uttar Pradesh, and Bihar) and lowest (∼25 μg/m3) in the southern and northeastern states. The NO2 exposure in Haryana, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, and West Bengal is 4–6 μg/m3, whereas exposure over Delhi NCR exceeds 17 μg/m3. The SMPI exposure across the IGP states like Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, and Bihar exceeds 20. Whereas moderate SMPI values of 12–24 are obtained across the states of Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, and West Bengal. Interestingly, we obtained the highest SMPI exposure of 60 over the Delhi NCR region, while most of the southern and northeastern states show lowest SMPI exposure (<5).

caption.p: Long‐term (2005–2019) annual mean PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI exposure for different states and Union Territories in India. The PM2.5 and NO2 exposure are in μg/m3, whereas SMPI exposure is unitless. The states are arranged in the descending order of SMPI values.

sec.p: We further examined the PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI exposure variability within the states by calculating exposure at the district level (Figure S6 in Supporting Information S1). Our findings reveal that PM2.5 exposure is highest across the IGP districts, with the pollutant concentration exceeding 60 μg/m3. PM2.5 level ranges between 30 and 45 μg/m3 or higher across most districts over northern India. The NO2 exposure distribution shows notable patterns. In addition to some districts in the IGP region, certain districts in Chhattisgarh, Orissa, and West Bengal (Eastern India) show elevated NO2 values (>8 μg/m3). Most districts in the IGP region possess higher SMPI exposure (>20). We found that several districts in Chhattisgarh and West Bengal also had enhanced SMPI values similar to that of IGP districts. The observed NO2 distribution contributes to these elevated SMPI levels in these regions.

We analyzed the percentage of population exposed to different species from 2005 to 2019 (Figure 3). The top left panel of the Figure 3 shows the population (in percentage) exposed to annual ambient PM2.5 concentrations and it indicates that the entire population of India is exposed to PM2.5 concentrations above the WHO's AQG of 5 μg/m3 throughout this period. Furthermore, there is a consistent rise in the number of people exposed to surface PM2.5 from 2005 to 2019. Notably, 40% of the population was exposed to >50 μg/m3 ambient PM2.5 in 2005, which has increased to 60% by 2019, highlighting a concerning trend in air quality and its impact on public health. The top right panel of Figure 3 indicates the population (in percentage) exposed to annual ambient NO2 concentrations, revealing a different pattern. The population exposed to higher NO2 levels (ranging from 2 to 4 μg/m3) has consistently increased from 2005 to 2014. However, a notable change occurred in 2015, as the population exposed to elevated NO2 levels decreased significantly (<2 μg/m3). This reduction may be due to some policies by National Green Tribunal (NGT), GOI and India's vehicular emissions Bharat Stage (BS)‐III and BS‐IV implemented on vehicles in 2005 and 2010 in major metro cities and in 2010 and 2017 countrywide (Singh et al., 2023).The findings by Biswal et al. (2025) also states that there is a decrease in tropospheric NO2 VCD over non‐attainments cities of India since 2017 which can be attributed to upgrading vehicular exhaust standards, electric vehicle usage, NCAP and other strict implementations. The bottom left panel of the figure shows the percentage of people exposed to SMPI from 2005 to 2019. This metric reflects the combined influence of PM2.5 and NO2 exposure. The percentage of people exposed to SMPI levels greater than 15 has increased from 2005 to 2014 from 10% to 21%. However, there is a slight shift in the pattern during 2015 as the population exposed to a higher level of SMPI (>15) has reduced to 16%. The figure reveals that the reduction in NO2 exposure caused this change in SMPI exposure.

caption.p: The population exposure (in %) to annual mean surface concentrations of PM2.5 (top left), NO2 (top right), and SMPI (bottom left) over India from 2005 to 2019 (years are represented by colors).

sec.p: We have analyzed the PM2.5, NO2 and SMPI changes across three distinct periods (2005–2009, 2010–2014, and 2015–2019; Figures S7 and S8 in Supporting Information S1). The Figure 4 consistently reveals an increase in the exposure to PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI in all states and UTs from 2010 to 2014 compared to 2005–2009. Maximum increase (10–40 (Unitless)) occurred in states like Delhi, Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, and Chhattisgarh. The changes in states such as Kerala, Arunachal Pradesh, Puducherry, Daman and Diu during 2010–2014 are minimal (<2). However, from 2015 to 2019, a notable decline in exposure to these pollutants is observed relative to the 2010–2014. States in the IGP, like Delhi, Punjab, Bihar, and Haryana, exhibit the most significant reductions. The maximum reduction in SMPI values (11–45.5) during 2015–2019 is obtained in states such as Delhi, Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, and Chandigarh. Analyzing the changes in PM2.5 and NO2 in all states and UTs during these periods, we can conclude that the reduction in both PM2.5 and NO2 exposure resulted in this. In Delhi, the reduction in PM2.5 (−38.5 μg/m3) and NO2 (−10 μg/m3) resulted in lower SMPI values, while in Chandigarh, the reduction in SMPI values (−11) is mainly contributed by the reduction in NO2 concentrations (−6.7 μg/m3). The results suggest that more changes have occurred in states and UTs in the IGP region and Eastern India (e.g., Delhi, Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh and Chandigarh) compared to other subregions.

caption.p: The changes in 5‐year average PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI exposure in Indian states and union territories (UTs) from 2010 to 2014 to 2015–2019 (left panel) and from 2005 to 2009 to 2010–2014 (right panel). The PM2.5 and NO2 exposure are in μg/m3, whereas SMPI exposure is unitless. The negative sign in the left panel indicates that the PM2.5, NO2 and SMPI exposure has reduced during 2015–2019 compared to 2010–2014.

sec.title: Urban‐Rural Variability of SMPI Exposure
sec.p: The distribution of India's population in urban (upper panel) and rural (lower panel) areas during different periods (2005–2009, 2010–2014, and 2015–2019) in relation to ambient PM2.5 and NO2 concentrations, and SMPI estimates is shown in Figure S9 of Supporting Information S1. The entire population in the country is exposed to PM2.5 levels higher than WHO air quality guidelines (5 μg/m3). The figure shows that approximately 13%–17% of the rural population is exposed to relatively low ambient PM2.5 concentrations (10–30 μg/m3). In contrast, a smaller proportion, ∼10% of the rural population is exposed to very high levels of ambient PM2.5 (90–110 μg/m3). However, most of the rural population is exposed to a range of 30–90 μg/m3. In contrast, analyzing PM2.5 exposure in urban areas reveals a different pattern. The maximum peak occurs within the 30–40 μg/m3 range, followed by another peak between 100 and 110 μg/m3. The distribution of population with respect to ambient NO2 concentrations shows that above 50% of the population (except urban population during 2010–2014) is exposed to a range of 0–2.5 μg/m3. The population exposed to higher values (3–6 μg/m3) is minimal (<20%) and more prevalent in urban areas during 2010–2014 (∼30%). The distribution of SMPI shows two distinct peaks in SMPI values of 5–10 and 20–30 in both rural and urban regions. The analysis reveals that SMPI exposure peaked during the 2010–2014 period compared to the adjacent pentads (2005–2009 and 2015–2019). The population distribution with respect to SMPI values shows a similar pattern of population in relation to PM2.5 values.

Figure 5 illustrates the changes in 5‐year average exposure to SMPI in the urban and rural areas of all states and union territories (UTs) in India from 2010 to 2014 to 2015–2019 (left panel) and from 2005 to 2009 to 2010–2014 (right panel). We have analyzed the changes in SMPI in the urban and rural areas of all states and UTs in India across three distinct periods (2005–2009, 2010–2014, and 2015–2019; Figures S10 and S11 in Supporting Information S1). The spatial distribution of 5‐year mean state‐wise SMPI values for different periods (Figure S10 in Supporting Information S1) shows that for most states, SMPI values in urban and rural areas are generally comparable. The figures show increased exposure to SMPI in the urban and rural areas of all states and UTs from 2010 to 2014 compared to 2005–2009. The urban regions of states and UTs in the IGP regions show more changes compared to rural areas. The urban areas in states like Delhi, Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal, and Chhattisgarh show maximum increase (SMPI values ranging from 4 to 22). While the rural areas in states and UTs like Andaman and Nicobar, Arunachal Pradesh, Daman and Diu, Kerala, Puducherry, Nagaland, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu show an increase in SMPI values (ranging from 3 to 12) compared to urban areas in these states. Due to India's regional scale air pollution problem (Dey et al., 2020; Karambelas et al., 2018; Ravishankara et al., 2020), the changes in SMPI exposure values in urban and rural areas of different states and UTs are comparable. Furthermore, from 2015 to 2019, a notable decline in SMPI exposure (values ranging from 6 to 27) can be observed in the urban areas of states like Delhi, Haryana, Chhattisgarh, and Punjab compared to the 2010–2014 period. Similarly, the reduction of SMPI exposure (4–12.5) in the rural areas was observed in states and UTs like Andaman and Nicobar, Karnataka, Puducherry, Arunachal Pradesh, Nagaland, and Kerala. In general, while urban areas, particularly those within the IGP region, experienced more pronounced changes in pollutant levels, rural areas in states like Kerala, Karnataka, Puducherry, and Nagaland also showed notable changes.

caption.p: The changes in 5‐year average SMPI exposure in urban and rural regions of Indian states and union territories (UTs) from 2010 to 2014 to 2015–2019 (left panel) and from 2005 to 2009 to 2010–2014 (right panel). The negative sign in the left panel indicates that the SMPI exposure has reduced during 2015–2019 compared to 2010–2014.

sec.title: Health Impact Assessment Using SMPI
sec.p: In this study, we also assessed the association between SMPI and the odds of COPD among Indian population (see Figure 6). Among overall population, we estimated larger association (per percentile increase in exposure) between PM2.5 and the odds of COPD prevalence (odds ratio of 1.093, 95% uncertainty intervals: 1.067–1.119) as compared to the NO2‐COPD association (1.022, 1.014–1.03). Moreover, the formulated SMPI estimate showed strong association with the respiratory health outcome (1.05, 1.025–1.075). We estimated consistent trends of increase in association between SMPI and the COPD prevalence as the air pollution exposure elevated for both the pollutants (Figure 6, left column). The increase in SMPI‐COPD association was higher from PM2.5 exposure as compared to that from NO2. For instance, the association between SMPI and COPD was 1.015 (1.005–1.025), 1.056 (1.036–1.076), 1.12 (1.104–1.136), and 1.167 (1.147–1.187), respectively, with the increase in PM2.5 exposure ranges of <25th, 25–50th, 50–75th, and >75th percentiles, adjusting for NO2. Similarly, with the increase in NO2 exposure ranges, the effect of SMPI increased (1.032 (1.024–1.04), 1.041 (1.033–1.049), 1.061 (1.045–1.077), and 1.094 (1.078–1.11)). More importantly, the net association between SMPI and COPD steeply increased as the exposure ranges increased for both the pollutants, relative to PM2.5 or NO2 separately. For instance, the association between SMPI and the odds of COPD increased as 1.03 (1.018–1.042), 1.073 (1.053–1.093), 1.131 (1.111–1.151), and 1.184 (1.156–1.212) with the increase in both PM2.5 and NO2 exposure ranges. The health effect assessment conducted in this study highlights that the effect of SMPI on the respiratory health outcome is intermediate when compared to the effect from each pollutant, respectively. This signifies that the effect may elevate by manifold among the populace living in higher PM2.5 and NO2 levels for COPD and other respiratory diseases. Conversely, India could prevent substantial health burden across the subregions if both pollutants are mitigated simultaneously, prioritizing their spatiotemporal distributions, source‐types, population density, or microenvironments.

caption.p: The association between SMPI exposures with the odds of COPD (left column) and its effect modification assessment for SMPI across the gender, age, wealth, case, cooking fuel types, and the resident types (right column). The circles denote the mean estimate of the association, and the whiskers depict the 95% uncertainty intervals (UIs). The term “pct” in the left panel is the abbreviation of “percentile.” We considered females, 25–35 years, rich, general, clean fuel users, and rural regions as reference while estimating the effect of SMPI across their demographic counterparts.

sec.p: The effect modification assessment (see Figure 6 right column) conducted in this study show strong association between SMPI and COPD among males (1.04, 1.028–1.052), solid‐fuel users (1.062, 1.049–1.075), and urban residents (1.056, 1.038–1.074). The association between SMPI and the odds of COPD increased consistently among older age groups (1.075 (1.062–1.088) in 35–45 years and 1.135 (1.112–1.158) in over 45 years). Moreover, we estimated larger SMPI‐COPD association among middle class (1.089, 1.076–1.102) and SC + ST subgroups (1.058, 1.05–1.066) as compared to poor (1.047, 1.035–1.059) and OBC (1.034, 1.013–1.055) sub‐population.

Table S2 in Supporting Information S1 shows the socio‐demographic characteristics of the survey. The wealthier subgroup was predominant in the urban regions, while the deprived ones (middle class and poor) in the rural areas. The general, OBC, and wealthier subgroups had adequate education and were professional workers; while the indigent sub‐populations had lower or inadequate education and were associated with farming or household activities. The prevalence of smoking and alcohol‐consumption were higher among males, poor, general, and OBC as compared to their demographic counterparts. The wealthier subgroups and general were associated with higher prevalence of unhealthy diet. The prevalence of COPD was higher among females, middle class, OBC, and older age groups as compared to their demographic counterparts. Among the overall population, mean (±CV: coefficient of variation, that is, the ratio of standard deviation to mean) SMPI estimate was 14.93 ± 0.638. The subgroups, male (15.14 ± 0.636), middle class (16.37 ± 0.62), poor (16.15 ± 0.491), general (16.26 ± 0.684), and OBC (14.72 ± 0.566) suffered from higher SMPI exposure as compared to their demographic counterparts.

We also estimated the health benefits attributable to meeting clean‐air targets (see Figure 7). During NFHS‐5 (2019‐21), COPD prevalence was higher (>1.2%) across the eastern, north‐eastern, and southern peninsular subregions. Whereas the moderate level of prevalence (0.6%–1.2%) was estimated over the western, central, and IGP regions (Figure 7a). Figure 7b shows that the subregions over the central and northern regions would obtain larger health benefits as compared to other parts of India, if the states reduce their PM2.5 and NO2 levels to meet their first clean‐air target. If the states keep on mitigating their air pollution levels to meet the second clean‐air target and eventually meet the WHO's AQG of 5 μg/m3 and 10 μg/m3, respectively, then most of the states could alleviate the COPD prevalence by a substantial amount (>40%) in the foreseeable future (see Figures 7c and 7d). We estimated that northern and central states would obtain larger health benefits as compared to the states over southern peninsular region. We noted that the high COPD prevalence for Kerala in the Figure 7d cannot be mis‐interpreted as no change in COPD prevalence even if this state meets its clean‐air targets for the two pollutants. Table S3 in Supporting Information S1 shows that the COPD prevalence would reduce from 4.17% during NFHS‐5 to 2.97%, 1.8%, and 1.49%, respectively, if the three clean‐air targets be met. Similarly, we estimated consistent reduction in COPD prevalence for Andhra Pradesh (2.62%–1.96%, 1.33%, and 0.45%), Meghalaya (2.65%–2%, 1.37%, and 0.38%), and Manipur (1.51%–1.14%, 0.78%, and 0.27%).

caption.p: The prevalence of COPD across the states during NFHS‐5 (a). The reduced COPD prevalence across the states if the three clean‐air targets are met for the foreseeable future (b–d). The color codes (blue to red) denote the increasing ranges of COPD prevalence. Table S3 in Supporting Information S1 documents the COPD prevalence estimates and the changes in SMPI across the states.

sec.title: Discussion and Conclusion
sec.p: In this study, we used high‐resolution satellite‐based PM2.5 from van Donkelaar et al. (2021) and NO2 from Cooper et al. (2022a, 2022b) to calculate SMPI exposure and associated health effects (COPD) over India. Our analysis of the SMPI exposure for each state, district, and urban‐rural area in India during this period has identified air pollution hotspots in various states and districts in Indo‐Gangetic Plain (IGP) and eastern India. The analysis of SMPI exposure for distinct periods reveals an increase in exposure to PM2.5, NO2, and SMPI in all states and UTs from 2010 to 2014 compared to the 2005–2009 period. The states and UTs in the IGP region showed large exposure changes. However, from 2015 to 2019, a notable decline in exposure to these pollutants was observed relative to the 2010–2014 period. The reduction in SMPI over recent years could be primarily attributed to a decrease in the ambient pollutant concentrations. The ambient PM2.5 exposure has reduced over the national clean air program (NCAP)‐era (post 2017) due to stricter implementation of air pollution mitigation policies, interventions, and action plans (Katoch et al., 2023). On the other hand, reduction in NO2 could be attributed to more robust adoption of electric vehicles (EVs), stricter monitoring of old vehicles and their renewal process, and accelerated usage of compressed natural gas (CNG) across the metropolitan cities and its peripheral subregions (Balakrishnan et al., 2019). Furthermore, the analysis of SMPI exposure in rural and urban areas for each state indicates that air pollution in India is not solely an urban problem. The SMPI provides better insights to pollution hotspots by the combined effect of pollution from PM2.5 and NO2 rather than a single pollutant. Note that the calculation of SMPI uses both the pollutants (PM2.5 and NO2) scaled by pollutant concentration deviation from WHO guidelines. Moreover, the satellite data used to calculate the SMPI offers high spatial and temporal coverage, which is a significant advantage over data from ground‐based monitoring systems. Our findings therefore demonstrate that the SMPI is a valuable tool for assessing air pollution levels and identifying areas needing air quality improvement.

Furthermore, we demonstrated the multi‐pollutant index approach to assess the adverse health impact of air pollution in India using the SMPI data set. Note that the development of health‐based metrics is emerging from the developed countries and showing potentials for epidemiological and toxicological research (Oakes et al., 2014). The results from our health effect assessment are comparable with a few contemporary studies in other countries. A recent cohort study in China revealed a higher impact of PM2.5 on health outcomes than NO2, while the effect was reduced in a multi‐pollutant model (Ji et al., 2022). Yang et al. (2022) reported a larger effect from PM2.5 than from NO2 over China in both single and multi‐pollutant models, where multiple health outcomes are considered. Another cohort‐based study has obtained mixed effects of these pollutants across the health outcomes and sub‐populations (Y. Wei et al., 2023). They also found that the combined effect from PM2.5 and NO2 was higher among males, older age groups, deprived sub‐population, and in urban regions.

Finally, we assessed the disparity in association between SMPI and COPD across the population subgroups in India. The heterogeneity in the association between SMPI and COPD across different PM2.5 and NO2 exposure ranges could be due to spatial heterogeneity in population and the exposure distribution of the pollutants. Ambient PM2.5 has larger spatial heterogeneity and more variation in its chemical compositions across the states (Chaudhary et al., 2022; Katoch et al., 2023; N. Singh et al., 2021). These may influence more varying results in the net SMPI‐COPD association. However, the variation in SMPI‐COPD association attributable to changes in NO2 exposure is less prominent as compared to changes in co‐pollutant. Note that ambient NO2 is a short‐lived criteria pollutant and possesses lower spatial heterogeneity (N. Singh et al., 2023). Therefore, NO2 may have lesser influence on the net association between SMPI and COPD. The presence of a significant interaction among these two pollutants dictates a complex relationship, when their exposure and associated health effects are considered among the populace. The biological mechanism of the effect of PM2.5 exposure and leading to adverse respiratory health outcomes is well established (Feng et al., 2016; Mack et al., 2019; T. Wei & Tang, 2018). Therefore, the disparity in health effects attributable to PM2.5 across the population subgroups in this study could be explained comprehensively. However, the exposure to ambient NO2 possesses larger uncertainty and substantial knowledge gaps (Mainka & Żak, 2022; Sun et al., 2024). Note that we estimated a larger effect from participants' age, gender, caste, resident type, status of availing health insurance, and information of having an unhealthy diet on the cause‐effect relationship (Figure S12 in Supporting Information S1). These socioeconomic and demographic factors influence subgroups' variability in mobility, source‐type and location, microenvironment, socioeconomic status, complex etiology of COPD, and outdoor‐to‐indoor concentration relationships of the pollutants (Venkataraman et al., 2018). For instance, men are more likely to spend more times outdoors and be exposed to outdoor occupational hazards, and more prevalent to tobacco smoking and unhealthy dietary intakes as compared to females (Chaudhary et al., 2022). These covariates might play significant roles in the inter‐relationships between NO2 and COPD association, and larger effects among males. The variation in the distribution of these covariates across the subregions and across diverse demography may have interdependent relationships with the health outcomes that modified the net association.

Most existing studies from India have estimated the single pollutant effect on various health outcomes (Chaudhary et al., 2023; Nori‐Sarma et al., 2021; Rajak & Chattopadhyay, 2020; Spears et al., 2019). Whereas this study shows that co‐exposure assessment could be an effective tool for robust air quality monitoring, managing strategies, and future comprehensive epidemiological studies. This study also shows that SMPI or similar multi‐pollutant indices may be more suitable for assessing air quality and related health impacts and identifying hotspots that require prioritized pollution mitigation. We emphasize on having mitigation policies and interventions targeting the vulnerable subgroups across the states, especially more attention is required for the southern peninsular region. Moreover, stricter air pollution mitigation mandates required to be implemented prioritizing males, middle class, OBC and tribal subgroups, and the older sub‐population. To reduce both the exposure burden and the associated risk of COPD among these subgroups, targeted, equity‐focused policies and interventions are essential, particularly in states and subregions where the demographic density of these populations is higher. The OBC and SC + ST communities are among the economically disadvantaged subgroups in India and require specific interventions, including access to cleaner fuels and improved healthcare services (Apte et al., 2017). Additionally, air pollution mitigation plans must be strengthened and tailored for regions with high densities of the middle‐class population, who may also face considerable exposure risks. Currently, India's environmental, health, and social policies adopt a universal approach, treating all population subgroups equally. Frameworks such as the NCAP have emphasized city‐level emission reduction targets but lack explicit provisions for addressing intra‐urban disparities or subgroup‐level inequities (Ganguly et al., 2020). We therefore recommend incorporating exposure equity indicators such as subgroup‐specific SMPI estimates into the performance assessments, policy prioritization, and funding allocation mechanisms of NCAP and state‐level action plans. Moreover, health outcomes are not yet integrated into the NCAP framework (Katoch et al., 2023). We suggest that the effectiveness of clean‐air interventions be assessed not only by pollution reduction but also by their anticipated health benefits. To achieve environmental and health equity and align with the Sustainable Development Goals, future environmental policies in India must directly address disparities in PM2.5 and NO2 exposure and their health consequences across different states and demographic subgroups.

Our study possesses a few limitations which warrant discussion. The demographic characteristics and health information (COPD prevalence) captured in the last census were used as the baseline for the sampling framework in recent NFHSs. We used the cluster‐specific sample weight in our analysis to make the assessment best representative of the changing demography of the population. However, the questionnaire‐response of COPD prevalence (Yes or No) was considered as the health outcome information in our assessment. Due to the lack of accurate biomarker measurement of COPD among the populace, the estimated prevalence could be under‐ or overestimated. The future NFHS sampling strategy based on the demographic distributions being captured in the ongoing census of India will further reduce uncertainty in demographic characteristics and COPD prevalence estimates. We found a reduction in the average ambient NO2 exposure in 2015 across India, which also led to a slight reduction in SMPI exposure and effected on the subsequent health effect assessment as well. Although a detailed investigation of the root cause of this decline was beyond the scope of our analysis, existing literature provided supporting evidence. The observed decline in population exposure to elevated NO2 levels in India around 2015 likely reflected a combination of early emission control efforts (vehicular and industrial), infrastructure and energy transitions, and favorable meteorological conditions. Singh et al. (2023) reported that the reduction in NO2 over India may have been linked to several policies implemented after 2009, including measures by the National Green Tribunal (NGT) and the rollout of Bharat Stage (BS)‐III and BS‐IV vehicular emission standards in 2005 and 2010 for major metro cities, and in 2010 and 2017 nationwide.

Finally, future research should incorporate more satellite‐derived criteria air pollutants (sulfur dioxide, ozone) and hazardous air pollutants (formaldehyde) in formulating the SMPI index to estimate the effect from the combination of these pollutants. The component toxicity should also be incorporated in the formulation of air quality metrics to lead such epidemiological assessments more comprehensive and supportive for robust policies and action plans. Future studies of multi‐pollutant models along with the effect modification from other environmental risk factors are required to establish the causal mechanisms among air pollutant mixtures and public health.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The satellite‐derived annual mean surface PM2.5 data (V5.GL.03) used in this study (van Donkelaar et al., 2021) and the high‐resolution surface NO2 data (Cooper et al., 2022a, 2022b) are publicly available from Washington University Atmospheric composition analysis Group website. The population data is provided by the WorldPop Research program (WorldPop & Bondarenko, 2020). The human settlement data set developed by the Joint Research Centre (JRC) of the European Commission and the Directorate‐General for Regional and Urban Policy is publicly available as part of the GHS‐SMOD R2022 A data package (Schiavina, Melchiorri, & Pesaresi, 2022; Schiavina, Melchiorri, Pesaresi, Politis, et al., 2022).

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The satellite‐derived annual mean surface PM2.5 data (V5.GL.03) used in this study (van Donkelaar et al., 2021) and the high‐resolution surface NO2 data (Cooper et al., 2022a, 2022b) are publicly available from Washington University Atmospheric composition analysis Group website. The population data is provided by the WorldPop Research program (WorldPop & Bondarenko, 2020). The human settlement data set developed by the Joint Research Centre (JRC) of the European Commission and the Directorate‐General for Regional and Urban Policy is publicly available as part of the GHS‐SMOD R2022 A data package (Schiavina, Melchiorri, & Pesaresi, 2022; Schiavina, Melchiorri, Pesaresi, Politis, et al., 2022).
